preprocessor.ambiguity_note: 'Best' is ambiguous; interpreted as most purchased tracks based on invoice line items.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show me the most purchased tracks in the database.

sql:
-- Interpreting "most purchased" as tracks with the highest count of invoice line items
SELECT 
    t.TrackId,
    t.Name AS track_name,
    ar.Name AS artist,
    al.Title AS album,
    g.Name AS genre,
    COUNT(il.InvoiceLineId) AS purchase_count,
    SUM(il.UnitPrice * il.Quantity) AS total_revenue
FROM Track t
JOIN Album al ON t.AlbumId = al.AlbumId
JOIN Artist ar ON al.ArtistId = ar.ArtistId
LEFT JOIN Genre g ON t.GenreId = g.GenreId
JOIN InvoiceLine il ON t.TrackId = il.TrackId
GROUP BY t.TrackId, t.Name, ar.Name, al.Title, g.Name
ORDER BY purchase_count DESC
LIMIT 20

sample_rows.album: For Those About To Rock We Salute You
For Those About To Rock We Salute You

input: Which employee is the most senior?

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: 'Most senior' is interpreted as the employee with the earliest hire date in the database.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Which employee has the earliest hire date?

sql:
-- Interpreting "most senior" as the employee with the earliest HireDate
SELECT e.EmployeeId,
       e.FirstName || ' ' || e.LastName AS full_name,
       e.Title,
       e.HireDate
FROM Employee e
ORDER BY e.HireDate ASC
LIMIT 1

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted 'popular' as genres with the highest number of tracks in the database. Could also mean genres with the most purchases/invoices.
preprocessor.normalized_query: What are the most popular genres by number of tracks or sales?

sql:
-- Interpreted 'popular' in two ways: (1) by track count, (2) by total sales revenue
-- Showing both metrics side by side for each genre
SELECT
    g.Name AS genre,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.TrackId) AS track_count,
    COUNT(il.InvoiceLineId) AS total_purchases,
    ROUND(SUM(il.UnitPrice * il.Quantity), 2) AS total_revenue
FROM Genre g
LEFT JOIN Track t ON g.GenreId = t.GenreId
LEFT JOIN InvoiceLine il ON t.TrackId = il.TrackId
GROUP BY g.GenreId, g.Name
ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
LIMIT 20

sql:

       c.FirstName,
       c.LastName,
       c.Email,
       c.City,
       c.State,

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted 'top sellin artsts' as top selling artists ranked by total revenue or number of tracks sold from invoices.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show the top selling artists by total sales.

sql:
-- Interpreting "top selling artists" as artists ranked by total revenue from invoice lines
SELECT ar.Name AS artist,
       SUM(il.UnitPrice * il.Quantity) AS total_sales,
       COUNT(il.InvoiceLineId) AS tracks_sold
FROM Artist ar
JOIN Album a ON ar.ArtistId = a.ArtistId
JOIN Track t ON a.AlbumId = t.AlbumId
JOIN InvoiceLine il ON t.TrackId = il.TrackId
GROUP BY ar.ArtistId, ar.Name
ORDER BY total_sales DESC
LIMIT 20

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Query is incomplete or unclear. Interpreted as a request to retrieve all tracks from the database.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show all tracks

sql:
SELECT t.TrackId,
       t.Name AS track_name,
       al.Title AS album,
       ar.Name AS artist,
       g.Name AS genre,
       mt.Name AS media_type,
       t.Composer,
       ROUND(t.Milliseconds / 60000.0, 2) AS duration_minutes,
       t.UnitPrice
FROM Track t
LEFT JOIN Album al ON t.AlbumId = al.AlbumId
LEFT JOIN Artist ar ON al.ArtistId = ar.ArtistId
LEFT JOIN Genre g ON t.GenreId = g.GenreId
LEFT JOIN MediaType mt ON t.MediaTypeId = mt.MediaTypeId
ORDER BY ar.Name, al.Title, t.TrackId
LIMIT 20

sql:

FROM Track t
JOIN Genre g ON t.GenreId = g.GenreId
JOIN Album a ON t.AlbumId = a.AlbumId
JOIN Artist ar ON a.ArtistId = ar.ArtistId
WHERE g.Name = 'Rock'
ORDER BY ar.Name, a.Title, t.Name
LIMIT 20

input: Customers who spent more than the average customer

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted 'spent' as total invoice amount paid by each customer; 'average customer' means the average total spend across all customers.
preprocessor.normalized_query: List customers whose total amount spent on invoices is greater than the average total spending across all customers.

sql:
-- Interpreting 'spent' as sum of invoice totals per customer;
-- 'average customer spending' as the mean of per-customer totals.
SELECT
    c.CustomerId,
    c.FirstName || ' ' || c.LastName AS customer_name,
    c.Email,
    c.Country,
    ROUND(SUM(i.Total), 2) AS total_spent
FROM Customer c
JOIN Invoice i ON c.CustomerId = i.CustomerId
GROUP BY c.CustomerId, c.FirstName, c.LastName, c.Email, c.Country
HAVING SUM(i.Total) > (
    SELECT AVG(customer_total)
    FROM (
        SELECT SUM(Total) AS customer_total
        FROM Invoice
        GROUP BY CustomerId
    ) AS avg_subquery
)
ORDER BY total_spent DESC

input: Artists who share a genre with Iron Maiden

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted as artists whose tracks belong to the same genre(s) as any track by Iron Maiden, excluding Iron Maiden itself.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Find all artists who have tracks in the same genre as Iron Maiden's tracks, excluding Iron Maiden itself.

sql:
-- Interpreted as: artists whose tracks belong to any genre that Iron Maiden also has tracks in, excluding Iron Maiden itself.
SELECT DISTINCT ar.ArtistId, ar.Name AS artist_name
FROM Artist ar
JOIN Album al ON ar.ArtistId = al.ArtistId
JOIN Track t ON al.AlbumId = t.AlbumId
WHERE t.GenreId IN (
    SELECT DISTINCT t2.GenreId
    FROM Artist ar2
    JOIN Album al2 ON ar2.ArtistId = al2.ArtistId
    JOIN Track t2 ON al2.AlbumId = t2.AlbumId
    WHERE ar2.Name = 'Iron Maiden'
)
AND ar.Name != 'Iron Maiden'
ORDER BY ar.Name
LIMIT 20

input: Employees who manage more staff than their own manager does

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted as: employees whose count of direct reports exceeds the count of direct reports of their own manager
preprocessor.normalized_query: Find employees who have more direct subordinates than their own manager has

sql:
-- Interpreted as: employees whose count of direct reports exceeds the count of direct reports of their own manager
SELECT
    e.EmployeeId,
    e.FirstName || ' ' || e.LastName AS employee_name,
    e.Title AS employee_title,
    emp_sub.direct_reports AS employee_direct_reports,
    m.FirstName || ' ' || m.LastName AS manager_name,
    m.Title AS manager_title,
    mgr_sub.direct_reports AS manager_direct_reports
FROM Employee e
JOIN Employee m ON e.ReportsTo = m.EmployeeId
JOIN (
    SELECT ReportsTo, COUNT(*) AS direct_reports
    FROM Employee
    WHERE ReportsTo IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY ReportsTo
) AS emp_sub ON emp_sub.ReportsTo = e.EmployeeId
JOIN (
    SELECT ReportsTo, COUNT(*) AS direct_reports
    FROM Employee
    WHERE ReportsTo IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY ReportsTo
) AS mgr_sub ON mgr_sub.ReportsTo = m.EmployeeId
WHERE emp_sub.direct_reports > mgr_sub.direct_reports
ORDER BY emp_sub.direct_reports DESC

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: "Last month" is relative to the current date; interpreted as the calendar month immediately preceding today's date.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show all invoices from the previous calendar month.

sql:
-- Using "last month" relative to the most recent data point (2025-12-22)
-- Previous calendar month = November 2025
SELECT
    i.InvoiceId,
    i.CustomerId,
    c.FirstName || ' ' || c.LastName AS customer_name,
    i.InvoiceDate,
    i.BillingAddress,
    i.BillingCity,
    i.BillingCountry,
    i.Total
FROM Invoice i
JOIN Customer c ON i.CustomerId = c.CustomerId
WHERE i.InvoiceDate >= '2025-11-01'
  AND i.InvoiceDate < '2025-12-01'
ORDER BY i.InvoiceDate ASC
LIMIT 20

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: The term 'recently added' is ambiguous as the database may not have a timestamp for when tracks were added. This will be interpreted as tracks with the highest TrackId values, assuming higher IDs were added later.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show the most recently added tracks, interpreted as tracks with the highest TrackId values.

sql:
-- Interpreting "recently added" as tracks with the highest TrackId values (no addition timestamp available)
SELECT 
    t.TrackId,
    t.Name AS track_name,
    a.Title AS album,
    ar.Name AS artist,
    g.Name AS genre,
    mt.Name AS media_type,
    t.Milliseconds / 1000 AS duration_seconds,
    t.UnitPrice
FROM Track t
LEFT JOIN Album a ON t.AlbumId = a.AlbumId
LEFT JOIN Artist ar ON a.ArtistId = ar.ArtistId
LEFT JOIN Genre g ON t.GenreId = g.GenreId
LEFT JOIN MediaType mt ON t.MediaTypeId = mt.MediaTypeId
ORDER BY t.TrackId DESC
LIMIT 20

input: Sales trend over the past year

preprocessor.ambiguity_note: Interpreted as total invoice amounts aggregated by month over the most recent 12-month period available in the database.
preprocessor.normalized_query: Show the sales trend (total revenue per month) over the past year based on invoice data.

sql:
-- Interpreting "past year" as the 12-month period ending at the most recent data point (2025-12-22)
-- Aggregating total invoice amounts by year-month
SELECT
    STRFTIME('%Y-%m', i.InvoiceDate) AS year_month,
    COUNT(i.InvoiceId)               AS invoice_count,
    ROUND(SUM(i.Total), 2)           AS total_revenue
FROM Invoice i
WHERE i.InvoiceDate >= '2025-01-01'
  AND i.InvoiceDate <= '2025-12-22'
GROUP BY year_month
ORDER BY year_month ASC
